Application of social force model to pedestrian behavior analysis
at signalized crosswalk
Weiliang Zeng  
, Peng Chen 
, Hideki Nakamura  
, Miho Iryo-Asano  
Show more
Outline
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.trc.2014.01.007
Get rights and content
Full text access
Highlights
•
Formulate pedestrians’ decision-making behavior at signalized
crosswalk.
•
Represent pedestrian evasion behavior and collision avoidance with
vehicles.
•
Calibrate model parameters by separating into measurable and non-
measurable groups.
•
Visually reproduce pedestrian crossing behavior in microscopic
simulation.
Abstract
Limited pedestrian behavior models shed light on the case at signalized crosswalk, where pedestrian behavior is characterized by
group or individual evasion with surrounding pedestrians, collision avoidance with conflicting vehicles, and response to signal
control and crosswalk boundary. This study fills this gap by developing a microscopic simulation model for pedestrian behavior
analysis at signalized intersection. The social force theory has been employed and adjusted for this purpose. The parameters,
including measurable and non-measurable ones, are either directly estimated based on observed dataset or indirectly derived by
maximum likelihood estimation. Last, the model performance was confirmed in light of individual trajectory comparison
between estimation and observation, passing position distribution at several cross-sections, collision avoidance behavior with
conflicting vehicles, and lane-formation phenomenon. The simulation results also concluded that the model enables to visually
represent pedestrian crossing behavior as in the real world.

Keywords
Pedestrian behavior; Social force; Microscopic traffic simulation; Signalized intersection
1. Introduction
The importance of modeling pedestrian behavior has been well recognized in both fields of safety and capacity assessment of
road facilities (Papadimitriou et al., 2009). In this regard, simulation can be a powerful tool for pedestrian behavior reproduction.
It has been successfully applied to human navigation analysis (Usher and Strawderman, 2010, Kneidl et al., 2013), pedestrian
facility assessment (Zhang et al., 2008, Maria et al., 2013) and crowd dynamic analysis (Hoogendoorn and Daamen, 2005, Helbing
et al., 2005). The simulation technology offers a flexible approach for detailed analysis of pedestrian behavior that may result in
safety or efficiency problem at road facilities.
This study is part of intensive efforts to develop a microscopic simulation model for safety assessment at signalized intersection.
Although signalized crosswalks are operated to give pedestrians prioritized right of way, more than 30% of the total traffic
accident fatalities in Japan are pedestrians at crosswalks (National Police Agency of Japan, 2013) whereas 15% in Germany
(German Institute for Economic Research, 2010). Many reasons exist behind such statistics, such as risk-taking behavior,
intersection geometry, and traffic signal control. Among these factors, stochastic pedestrian behavior is deemed as a critical
factor that may result in safety problems. In order to develop such a simulation model, pedestrian behavior must be reasonably
represented.
To date, pedestrian behavior modeling has attracted considerable attention. With comparison to macroscopic models,
microscopic models provide a more detailed description of pedestrian behavior. Generally, existing microscopic models can be
classified into two categories: discrete and continuum models.
The family of discrete models essentially includes discrete choice model (Antonini et al., 2006, Robin et al., 2009, Guo and Huang,
2012), lattice gas model (Helbing et al., 2003, Guo and Huang, 2008) and cellular automaton model (Burstedde et al., 2001,
Kirchner and Schadschneider, 2002), in which space is discretized to approximate real pedestrian movement. It is convenient to
set various rules in a discrete model, such as direction keeping, destination orientation, free-flow acceleration, leader–follower
maneuver, and collision avoidance. However, only limited choices for speed and direction in a discrete choice model and limited
cellular alternatives in a cellular automaton model are provided, which is difficult to represent the flexible pedestrian behavior,
especially for the interactive case in the real world.
Continuum models use differential equations to describe the dynamic movement in space. At the early stage, a magnetic-force
model was developed by borrowing a motion equation used for magnetic fields (Okazaki, 1979). Based on this concept, a more
robust physical force based model, i.e., social force model, was applied to evacuation analysis (Helbing and Molnar, 1995). A
comparison overview of crowd motion simulation models shows that the force based model has many advantages (Duives et al.,
2013). Firstly, it enables to describe the dynamic movement of pedestrian in space and time. Secondly, each equation and
parameter can be easily explained from the viewpoint of kinematics. Thirdly, it allows reproducing the typical features of
pedestrian flow, such as lane formation and oscillation, due to the interaction with other pedestrians (Helbing et al., 2000,
Helbing et al., 2001). Although numerous studies based on social force model focused on the general mechanism of pedestrian
behavior, few of them shed light on the application of social force model to pedestrian behavior at signalized crosswalk. Teknomo
(2006) developed a social force based model and analyzed the pedestrian behavior of one-way and two-way pedestrian flow at
crosswalk. It was found that two-way traffic performance reduces significantly as the number of pedestrians increase. Li et al.
(2012) modified the social force model by considering the required space and the critical gaps with turning vehicles, which
makes it possible to describe the stop/go decision to the conflicting vehicles. However, these models, neither fully developed nor
calibrated based on real trajectory data, failed to illustrate the crucial pedestrian behavior at crosswalk.
To fill the gap, the special characteristics of pedestrian behavior at signalized crosswalk are considered in this study. It takes into
account the crossing behaviors such as group evasive and individual evasive behavior with surrounding pedestrians, collision
avoidance with turning vehicles, reaction to crosswalk boundary, and response to signal control, which are all important to

evaluate the safety performance of signalized intersections. Then, a statistical method is applied to parameter calibration. In this
study, the parameters are separated into two groups, measurable and non-measurable ones. The measurable parameters are
directly estimated from the observed trajectory dataset, and the non-measurable parameters are derived by using maximum
likelihood estimation (MLE). Last, the results of simulation scenarios conclude that the model enables to represent the typical
characteristics of pedestrian behavior at signalized crosswalk such as lane-formation phenomenon, collision avoidance with
conflicting vehicles and surrounding pedestrians.
2. Model framework
As shown in Fig. 1, the general structure of the model is based on the decision making level of psychological process. The first
level is strategic level, at which pedestrians make a decision of the intended direction. The second level is operational level,
which is the second step for pedestrians to adjust their walking behavior at the crosswalk. Note that after reacting to the
pedestrian signal phase and deciding to enter the crosswalk, pedestrians will adjust their speeds and directions dynamically
when interacting with other road users and crosswalk facilities.

Fig. 1. Psychological process of pedestrian behavior.
3. Desired direction model
The psychological process of decision-making of desired direction is like the route-choice strategy. As shown in Fig. 2, there are
four OD zones (N1, N2, F1, F2) defined for pedestrian crossing. Accordingly, there are three stages for a pedestrian finishing the
crossing, i.e., from origin to crosswalk entering position, from crosswalk entering position to crosswalk exit position, and last
from crosswalk exit position to destination. It is assumed that the desired entering position at crosswalk is determined once the
pedestrians enter the waiting area and the desired exit position is determined once the pedestrian enter the crosswalk. Thus, the
desired direction at waiting area (stage ①) is defined as the vector pointing from current position to the desired entering position,
and the desired direction at crosswalk (stage ②) is defined as the vector pointing from the current position to the desired exit
position. Since the focus of this study is pedestrian walking behavior at crosswalk, only the second stage is discussed in the
following sections.

influenced by crosswalk geometry, pedestrian origin–destination movement, previous passing position and the densities of other

applied to estimate the desired entering and exit positions of pedestrians. Thus, the probability density function of passing
position distribution is established by using Weibull distribution as shown in Eq. (1) (Zeng et al., 2013).

4. Stop/go decision model
It is assumed that all the pedestrians choose to go when the signal phase turns to green and to stop entering the crosswalk once
the signal phase turns to red. However, pedestrian flashing green (PFG) is a special signal phase which is considered as a
clearance time interval for pedestrians to safely finish walking at crosswalk. In Japan, although pedestrians are not supposed to
enter the crosswalk during PFG by law, many of them rush into the crosswalk without taking care of approaching turning
vehicles, which may cause higher probability to get severe conflicts with the vehicles. Thus, a stop/go decision model is necessary
to present the pedestrian crossing behavior at PFG. According to the pedestrian crossing behavior model by Asano et al. (2013),
the probability of stop/go is related to the walking speed and the distance from the current position to the crosswalk at the onset
of PFG. In the binary Logit-based model, the error term of utility function to choose “go” is assumed to follow Gumbel
distribution and the probability to go is represented by Eq. (2).

again within a certain relaxation time. This force is called driving force as shown in Eq. (6).

5.3. Force from surrounding pedestrians
As shown in Fig. 4, it is assumed that two kinds of collision avoidance behavior with surrounding pedestrians are related, i.e.,
group evasive and individual evasive behavior. When conflicting with others, pedestrians usually adjust their speed vectors to
avoid conflict with the pedestrian group from the opposite direction first. This behavior may result in self-organization such as
lane formation phenomena. Then, pedestrians will continue to avoid collision with the nearest conflicting pedestrian in the
private sphere, which cause individual avoidance behavior such as deceleration or turning direction to keep a safe distance with
each other. Thus, the force from surrounding pedestrians

5.3.1. Group evasive behavior
As shown in Fig. 4(b), it is assumed that each conflicting pedestrian within the visual range will generate an elliptical force field
resulting in the repulsive effect to the subject pedestrian. Referring to the classical social force model (Helbing and Molnar, 1995),
the repulsive potential is an exponential decreasing function of b  with equipotential lines having the form of an ellipse directed
into the direction of motion. b  is the length of the semi-minor axis of the elliptical force field generated by the conflicting
pedestrian i. Thus, the resultant force from the conflicting pedestrian group can be presented by Eq. (10).

5.3.2. Individual evasive behavior
It is assumed that the subject pedestrian will implement a giving-way maneuver (Asano et al., 2010) to the nearest conflicting
pedestrian if someone enters the private sphere and a potential collision is going to occur. Fig. 4(c) shows two kinds of conflicts,
however, only the valid conflict may lead to collision. The valid conflict can be identified by the time to conflict point (TTCP).
TTCP is defined as the expected time to pass the conflict point if the two pedestrians remain at their current speeds and
directions. The TTCP for the subject pedestrian α and the conflicting pedestrian β is given by Eq. (11). Such conflicts that the
TTCPs of both pedestrians are positive will be deemed as valid, whereas a negative value means that one pedestrian have passed

Once a valid conflict is identified, a giving-way maneuver will be performed. As shown in Fig. 4(d), the subject pedestrian takes
actions to avoid collisions with the nearest conflicting pedestrian β by adjusting the speed or direction. A lack of giving-way
maneuver may lead to a problem that two pedestrians may stop forever (when TTCP  → +∞ and TTCP  → +∞) due to social force
balance. For instance, when two conflicting pedestrians in opposite directions move in a straight line, the social force balance
may occur if no other disturbances come. Thus, an active mechanism to avoid such balance conditions is necessary. The giving-
way maneuver is flexible and stochastic in the real world. Pedestrians can pass the nearest conflicting pedestrian smoothly by
adopting either accelerating or decelerating maneuver. Meanwhile, direction changing or not is dependent on personal
preference. To reflect the characteristic of the giving-way maneuver, a stochastic adjustment mechanism is developed. Fig. 5
shows the process of finding out a reasonable speed changing or direction changing to avoid the collision. If T

Since it is left-hand traffic in Japan, left-turning vehicles usually conflict with pedestrians at a permitted phase. Generally, drivers
are required to yield to crossing pedestrians. However, some drivers may take risky behavior by accepting short gaps and
threaten the safety of pedestrians. It is assumed that pedestrians are affected by the vehicle force field once he/she approaches it.
As shown in Fig. 6, the subject pedestrian will take actions to avoid collision with the left-turning vehicle although the vehicle
may yield to the pedestrian. This situation is similar to collision avoidance with conflicting pedestrians, though the physical size
of a left-turning vehicle is much larger than that of a pedestrian. Similarly, an elliptical force field is assumed to describe the
force generated by the nearest left-turning vehicle. The difference from the force field of a conflicting pedestrian is that the two
focuses of the ellipse are defined by the vehicle near-side rear position

5.5. Attractive force from signal
Pedestrian behavior is also influenced by the attractive force from signal control. Empirical analyses indicate that pedestrian
speeds are significantly higher at pedestrian flashing green (PFG) phase than those at pedestrian green phase (PG). The major
reason is that pedestrians need to finish crossing before the onset of the red signal as soon as possible. Here, it is assumed that if
the pedestrian is still at the crosswalk when the signal has changed to PFG or Red, he/she will accelerate and cross as fast as
possible. As shown in Fig. 7, the magnitude of the force is assumed to linearly relate to the current distance to the desired exit
position. The farther pedestrians are from the desired exit position, the stronger the force will be. However, if the current speed
has achieved the maximum acceptable speed, the force should be zero. Thus, the attractive force

6. Model calibration
6.1. Data acquisition
As shown in Fig. 8, the north crosswalk at Kanayama intersection in Nagoya City of Japan was chosen as study site. The positions
of pedestrians and left-turning vehicles were manually extracted from the video every 0.5 s by using an image processing
software (Suzuki and Nakamura, 2006). The dataset consists of the trajectories of 1352 pedestrians and 124 left-turning vehicles.
In total, 97,200 position samples are available for trajectory profiles based on time series. From these data, all relevant variables
can be derived either directly or by applying finite differences, such as positions 
, velocities 
, accelerations
, distances between pedestrians, and speed angles between pedestrians.

6.2. Calibration methodology
At the early stage, model parameters are commonly calibrated by comparing aggregate outcomes such as speed–density
relationship (Seyfried et al., 2006), or emerging pedestrian movement patterns such as lane formation (Helbing et al., 2000).
However, it is still unclear whether a microscopic model is able to represent individual walking behavior accurately with a
macroscopic calibration method, considering the fact that aggregate outcomes only provide a reasonable average prediction
(Hoogendoorn and Daamen, 2006). Recently, as the calibration of microscopic pedestrian models has been considered as an
important issue, some researchers have calibrated social force models by the maximum log-likelihood estimation method which
is able to describe the parameters in a statistical way (Daamen and Hoogendoorn, 2012, Ko et al., 2013). The calibration
methodology adopted in this study is similar to the one used by Hoogendoorn and Daamen (2006). However, there exist two
major differences. First, considering that acceleration is the second difference of the position, to estimate acceleration will
amplify the error in trajectory processing. Thus, this study uses the raw positions of trajectory rather than the estimated
acceleration for calibration. Secondly, the parameters are separated into two groups, i.e., measurable parameters and non-
measurable parameters, and calibrated respectively.
The developed social force model contains various parameters. As some of them cannot be derived from the observed dataset,
direct calibration is difficult. Here, we adopt a three-stage process to alleviate this problem. As the first step, the parameters such
as visual range, which are measurable but difficult to be identified from the trajectory dataset, are estimated by referring to
related studies. Secondly, the parameters such as relaxation time, which are possible to be identified from the observed dataset,
are directly estimated. Finally, after fixing the above parameters, other parameters, such as the strength coefficients of each force
that do not have concrete physical meanings, are derived by MLE.
6.2.1. Calibration of measurable parameter
Altogether, there are seven measurable parameters in the developed social force model, i.e., angle of visual range (θ), radius of
visual range (R), desired speed (
), relaxation time (τ ), maximum acceptable speed (v
), velocity change (Δv) and direction
change (ψ). The visual range is defined as the radius and angle at which a given standard object can be seen with unaided eyes.
Due to the limitation of data collection, it is difficult to directly estimate the visual range based on the trajectory dataset. Here,
according to related studies (Guo et al., 2010, Antonini et al., 2006), the angle of visual range is set as 170° and the radius of visual
range is set as 8 m.
Desired speed is defined as the personal average speed if a pedestrian crosses the road without any disturbance by signal,
boundary, surrounding pedestrians and vehicles. The trajectory data were collected when there were no conflicting pedestrians
and left-turning vehicles at pedestrian green phases and the subject pedestrian must be inside the crosswalk, which guarantees
less repulsive or attractive force influencing the subject pedestrian. As shown in Fig. 9(a), the results show that the average value
of desired speed is 1.6 m/s and the standard deviation is 0.15 m/s.

Fig. 9. Calibration results of measurable parameters.
Relaxation time is defined as the time for a pedestrian recovering from current speed to the personal desired speed without any
disturbance. To reduce the influence of other forces, only the dataset for desired speed estimation was used. However, the
pedestrian speeds still change dynamically due to personal random decision. Here, the relaxation time is estimated as the
acceleration period from a lower current speed to the personal desired speed. As shown in Fig. 10, if the current speed is lower
than the desired speed, a loop will start up to search forward and find out how many time steps were needed until the speed
recovers to the desired speed. As shown in Fig. 9(b), the average value is 2.2 s and the standard deviation is 0.5 s.
Download: Download full-size image
Fig. 10. Flow chart of relaxation time estimation.
Maximal acceptable speed is defined as the maximum instant speed along one pedestrian trajectory. Here, all of the trajectories
are utilized as the estimation dataset. As shown in Fig. 9(c), the average of maximal acceptable speeds is 1.8 m/s and the standard
deviation of maximal acceptable speeds is 0.3 m/s.
Velocity change is defined as the speed difference between the quantities of the current speed and the speed at next time
interval when the subject pedestrian interacts with the nearest conflicting pedestrian in the private sphere. To reduce other
influences, the trajectory data collection meets the following requirements. First, there was only one conflicting pedestrian and
he/she was in the private sphere of the subject pedestrian. Second, no left-turning vehicles exist. Third, it was at pedestrian green
phases. Finally, the subject pedestrian must be inside the crosswalk. As shown in Fig. 9(d), the average value is −0.07 m/s and the
standard deviation is 0.09 m/s.
Direction change is the angle difference between the current speed vector and the speed at next time interval when the subject
pedestrian interacts with the nearest conflicting pedestrian in the private sphere. The data collection condition is the same as the
condition of velocity change. As shown in Fig. 9(e), the average value is 0.03° and the standard deviation is 2.14°.

7. Model performance
To verify the performance, the developed model was incorporated into a microscopic simulation platform (Dang et al., 2012), in
which the vehicle behavior model had been incorporated. The pedestrian behavior is simulated for a large number of interacting
pedestrians confronted with different situations at signalized intersection.
7.1. Individual trajectory in simulation
To analyze the performance of the developed model, one of the pedestrian trajectories was extracted from the observed and
estimated dataset, respectively. The sample pedestrian was simulated while keeping the other pedestrians on their original
trajectories.
As shown in Fig. 11(a), the force from surrounding pedestrians has the most significant influence (an average of about 66.1% of
the resultant force during the whole period) on the pedestrian behavior because the tested pedestrian conflicts with others
during the whole trip. The force from vehicle is zero at the beginning. However, from the 8th second to the 14th second, the
subject pedestrian conflicts with a left-turning vehicle. During this period, the force from conflicting vehicle accounts for an
average of 15.3%. The driving force is around 18.5% of the resultant force during the whole period, providing the power to recover
to the desired speed toward destination. The force from boundary is about 12.4% of the resultant force, which is relatively stable
during the whole crossing process. The force from signal is zero because the crossing behavior happened at pedestrian green
phase.

Fig. 11(b) shows the comparison between the estimated and observed positions of the total crossing trajectory. It was found that
the Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) of the position estimation in each time step is 5.7%. Fig. 11(c) shows the comparison
between the estimated and observed speeds in time series. The MAPE of speed estimation is 10.5%, a little bit larger than that of
position estimation. One possible reason is that speed is the difference of the position and further amplifies the estimation error.
7.2. Passing position at three cross-sections
Fig. 12 shows the distribution of observed and simulated pedestrian trajectories from N2 to F2 at Kanayama North crosswalk.
Note that N1, N2, F1 and F2 denote the OD of pedestrian movement. No significant difference of passing positions is found at the
three representative cross-sections according to two sample t-test for difference of their means at the 95% confidence interval. In
addition, it indicates that the distribution of passing position is closely related to pedestrian OD movements. For example, in the
case of near-side passing position, pedestrians with the origin N2 tend to enter the crosswalk close to the left boundary of
crosswalk (near N2). Then, the peak of the histogram at middle cross-section shifts a little bit to the right side, indicating that
pedestrians have change their speed vectors when interacting with other road users. Finally, the peak of the histogram at far-side
cross-section further shifts to the left since pedestrians are appealed by the destination (F2). This validation demonstrates that
the desired direction model and driving force model can reasonably reflect the pedestrian behavior characteristic at crosswalk.

Fig. 12. Verification of estimated pedestrian passing positions at three cross-sections.
7.3. Collision avoidance with turning vehicle
To analyze the interaction with left-turning vehicles, the pedestrian passing position at a specified cross-section is chosen for
validation under two following conditions. One is pedestrian vs. vehicle conflicting condition with turning vehicles existing at or
approaching the crosswalk when pedestrians are crossing, whereas the other is no conflict condition. The specified cross-section,
i.e., the central line of the third vehicle lane (from the curb of the exit approach), is chosen for analysis, considering the
pedestrian vs. vehicle conflict occurs most frequently along this exit lane according to the observed dataset. Fig. 13 shows the
comparison of observed and estimated passing positions at the specified cross-section. It is shown that the peak of the histogram
in observed dataset shifts from 0 to 2 m if the pedestrian comes across left-turning vehicles. This phenomenon indicates that
pedestrians are likely to detour to avoid collision with vehicles. According to t-test at the 95% confidence interval, no significant
difference was found between observed and estimated passing positions. It demonstrates that the developed model can well
present the behavior of collision avoidance with vehicles. Note that the peak of the histogram in estimated dataset shifts from 0
to 2.5 m, indicating that the reaction of pedestrian in simulation is more sensitive than that in real situation. Thus, the repulsive
force from turning vehicle still needs to be improved in the future development of the model.

Fig. 13. Verification of collision avoidance with turning vehicles.

7.4. Self-organization phenomenon
Self-organization is one of the important indicators to verify whether the simulation actually produces real world phenomena or
not. A representative self-organization phenomenon is lane-formation. Due to the impact of various forces from surrounding
environment, the lanes of pedestrian flow will form dynamically and their numbers may depend on OD movement, pedestrian
density and crosswalk width, etc. Two simulation models were applied to examine the lane-formation phenomenon. As shown in
Fig. 14(a), one is route-choice based desired direction model, given that the individual decision-making of desired direction is like
the route-choice strategy. The desired direction is determined by Eq. (1) which is related to various influencing factors such as OD
movement and pedestrian density. The other is non-route-choice based desired direction model. As shown in Fig. 14(b), without
considering any influence from surrounding environment, a uniform random distribution is applied to generate the desired
direction.
Download: Download full-size image
Fig. 14. Desired direction settings (example: N1 → F2).
Fig. 15 shows the lane-formation phenomenon in the real world and in simulation. It demonstrates that the modified social force
model is capable of reflecting the significant self-organization phenomenon in simulation, though the shape of the formed lanes
is not exactly the same. Comparing to the lane-formation by using non-route-choice based model, the pedestrian group in the
same moving direction develops with a larger size and the length of the formed pedestrian lane appears longer by using route-
choice based model. Moreover, the lane forms easier and keeps a relatively stable shape for a longer period when the route-
choice based model is applied. In contrast, the formed lanes by using non-route-choice based model are smaller and unstable,
which are easier to break down.

Download: Download full-size image
Fig. 15. Lane-formation phenomenon in the real world and in simulation.
To compare the pedestrian flow performance between the route-choice based model and non-route-choice based model, various
performance indices such as the number of formed lanes, the instant angle between current direction and desired direction, and
the passing time at crosswalk, are analyzed.
The comparison of the number of formed lanes is shown in Fig. 16(a). The number of formed lanes was collected manually when
two opposite pedestrian flows begin to come across and the segregation phenomenon occurs. The average number of formed
lanes is 2.8 in observed dataset while it is 3.5 in simulation by using route-choice based model and 4.1 by using non-route-choice
based model. It demonstrates that the number of formed lanes by route-choice based model is closer to the observed value.
Download: Download full-size image
Fig. 16. Pedestrian flow performance.
Fig. 16(b) shows the distribution of the instant angle between current direction and desired direction. The instant angle was
collected in each time step. Zero degree means the pedestrian move toward the desired passing position straight. The angle
variation can reflect the frequency of interactions with conflicting pedestrians or other factors. According to the simulation
result, most of the pedestrians keep their directions toward the desired destination or only change a small angle in each time
step, which agrees with the statistics in observed dataset. However, the angle variation is larger by using non-route-choice
model. It demonstrates that the interaction among conflicting pedestrians is relatively severe because of the unstable lane-

formation. The performance of instant angle distribution is closer to the real situation by using route-choice based model, since
the desired direction can be reproduced by a reasonable distribution function in simulation, which helps to represent lane-
formation phenomenon as in the real world.
Fig. 16(c) shows the distribution of the passing time at crosswalk. It was found that there are about 90% of the pedestrians
finishing crossing within 30 s in observed dataset while it is about 86% by using route-choice based model and 76% by using non-
route-choice based model in simulation. The performance of passing time gets improved by using route-choice based model. One
possible reason can be attributed to the fact that the lane-formation is more stable. In reality, it is easier for pedestrians to cross
the road by following someone in lane-formation situation than moving against opposite flows. Therefore, the passing times by
using route-choice based model are shorter when compared to those by using non-route-choice based model.
8. Conclusions and future work
Based on pedestrian psychological process and the social force theory, a modified social force model considering the typical
pedestrian behavior at signalized intersection, e.g., group evasive and individual evasive maneuver, collision avoidance with
turning vehicles, and reaction to crosswalk boundary was developed. Then, the parameters of the developed model were
calibrated in a statistical way. The measurable parameters were directly estimated based on the observed data and the non-
measurable ones were derived by maximum likelihood estimation. Finally, the model performance was verified by various terms,
such as instant position, instant speed, pedestrian trajectory, collision avoidance behavior with conflicting vehicles, and lane-
formation phenomenon. The major contributions of this study are summarized as follows.
(1)
The desired direction model was developed for driving force. Most of the studies so far commonly assumed
simplified directions of OD as desired directions and the influencing factors such as crosswalk geometry and
pedestrian density were seldom considered. This study developed a desired direction model by estimating
the desired exit position at the edge of crosswalk, which enables to represent the pedestrian maneuver
toward a predetermined position more realistically. It is considered as an important step to approximate the
pedestrian trajectory at crosswalk. The verification of passing positions at three cross-sections showed no
significant difference between estimated and observed trajectories, which demonstrated that the desired
direction model and driving force model can reasonably reflect the pedestrian behavior characteristic at
crosswalk.
(2)
The typical stop/go decision-making behavior at PFG phase was incorporated, whereas the classical social
force model paid less attention upon this. The model was intended to represent the risk-taking crossing
behavior at PFG, which is crucial to pedestrian safety assessment at signalized intersections.
(3)
Not only repulsive effect, but also attractive effect were considered in the force from crosswalk boundary.
Considering that the crosswalk boundary is different from a rigid wall, pedestrians may walk outside the
boundary when the conflicts with other pedestrians become severe due to increasing density. Classical social
force from the border only presents the repulsive effect, which makes the pedestrians always inside a
walkway. The newly developed force from crosswalk boundary not only maintains the repulsive effect, but
also provides an attractive effect to those pedestrians outside the crosswalk.
(4)
A giving-way maneuver was modeled for individual evasion. The stochastic adjustment mechanism by speed
changing and direction changing was developed, which enables to effectively solve the problems in
simulation that two conflicting pedestrians may collide or stop forever (TTCP  → +∞ and TTCP  → +∞) due to
social force balance.
(5)
The force from conflicting left-turning vehicle was modeled. Avoiding collision with conflicting vehicle is one
of the typical pedestrian behavior at crosswalk. Similar to the repulsive force from pedestrian group, an
elliptical force field was assumed to describe the force generated by the nearest left-turning vehicle. The
verification of collision avoidance with turning vehicle showed that the distribution of passing position at a
specified cross-section is able to reflect the repulsive effect by conflicting vehicle.

(6)
The attractive force from PFG or Red phase was developed. This force is generated when the signal changes to
PFG or Red. The magnitude of the force was assumed to linearly relate to the current distance to the desired
exit position. The farther pedestrians are from the desired exit position, the stronger the force will be. This
attractive force can reflect the real phenomenon that pedestrians prefer to finish crossing as soon as possible
when he/she perceives potential risk.
(7)
In addition, this study provides a more detailed procedure for model calibration by distinguishing measurable
and non-measurable parameters. It was demonstrated to be an effective way to achieve favorable calibration
results.
(8)
The analysis of lane-formation phenomenon demonstrates that the modified social force model is capable of
reflecting the typical characteristic of self-organization in simulation. Moreover, the desired direction model
proposed by this study helps to improve the pedestrian flow performance at crosswalk.
However, there are still several problems needing further attention in future work. For example, the reaction of pedestrians to
vehicles is more sensitive in simulation than in real world. In addition, the pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics such as age,
gender, and partner relationship should also be considered as the influencing factors of crossing behavior. These limitations will
be improved by a more elaborate modeling approach and a more strict calibration procedure in our future work.

Abstract
Limited pedestrian behavior models shed light on the case at signalized crosswalk, where pedestrian behavior is characterized by
group or individual evasion with surrounding pedestrians, collision avoidance with conflicting vehicles, and response to signal
control and crosswalk boundary. This study fills this gap by developing a microscopic simulation model for pedestrian behavior
analysis at signalized intersection. The social force theory has been employed and adjusted for this purpose. The parameters,
including measurable and non-measurable ones, are either directly estimated based on observed dataset or indirectly derived by
maximum likelihood estimation. Last, the model performance was confirmed in light of individual trajectory comparison
between estimation and observation, passing position distribution at several cross-sections, collision avoidance behavior with
conflicting vehicles, and lane-formation phenomenon. The simulation results also concluded that the model enables to visually
represent pedestrian crossing behavior as in the real world.

Keywords
Pedestrian behavior; Social force; Microscopic traffic simulation; Signalized intersection
1. Introduction
The importance of modeling pedestrian behavior has been well recognized in both fields of safety and capacity assessment of
road facilities (Papadimitriou et al., 2009). In this regard, simulation can be a powerful tool for pedestrian behavior reproduction.
It has been successfully applied to human navigation analysis (Usher and Strawderman, 2010, Kneidl et al., 2013), pedestrian
facility assessment (Zhang et al., 2008, Maria et al., 2013) and crowd dynamic analysis (Hoogendoorn and Daamen, 2005, Helbing
et al., 2005). The simulation technology offers a flexible approach for detailed analysis of pedestrian behavior that may result in
safety or efficiency problem at road facilities.
This study is part of intensive efforts to develop a microscopic simulation model for safety assessment at signalized intersection.
Although signalized crosswalks are operated to give pedestrians prioritized right of way, more than 30% of the total traffic
accident fatalities in Japan are pedestrians at crosswalks (National Police Agency of Japan, 2013) whereas 15% in Germany
(German Institute for Economic Research, 2010). Many reasons exist behind such statistics, such as risk-taking behavior,
intersection geometry, and traffic signal control. Among these factors, stochastic pedestrian behavior is deemed as a critical
factor that may result in safety problems. In order to develop such a simulation model, pedestrian behavior must be reasonably
represented.
To date, pedestrian behavior modeling has attracted considerable attention. With comparison to macroscopic models,
microscopic models provide a more detailed description of pedestrian behavior. Generally, existing microscopic models can be
classified into two categories: discrete and continuum models.
The family of discrete models essentially includes discrete choice model (Antonini et al., 2006, Robin et al., 2009, Guo and Huang,
2012), lattice gas model (Helbing et al., 2003, Guo and Huang, 2008) and cellular automaton model (Burstedde et al., 2001,
Kirchner and Schadschneider, 2002), in which space is discretized to approximate real pedestrian movement. It is convenient to
set various rules in a discrete model, such as direction keeping, destination orientation, free-flow acceleration, leader–follower
maneuver, and collision avoidance. However, only limited choices for speed and direction in a discrete choice model and limited
cellular alternatives in a cellular automaton model are provided, which is difficult to represent the flexible pedestrian behavior,
especially for the interactive case in the real world.
Continuum models use differential equations to describe the dynamic movement in space. At the early stage, a magnetic-force
model was developed by borrowing a motion equation used for magnetic fields (Okazaki, 1979). Based on this concept, a more
robust physical force based model, i.e., social force model, was applied to evacuation analysis (Helbing and Molnar, 1995). A
comparison overview of crowd motion simulation models shows that the force based model has many advantages (Duives et al.,
2013). Firstly, it enables to describe the dynamic movement of pedestrian in space and time. Secondly, each equation and
parameter can be easily explained from the viewpoint of kinematics. Thirdly, it allows reproducing the typical features of
pedestrian flow, such as lane formation and oscillation, due to the interaction with other pedestrians (Helbing et al., 2000,
Helbing et al., 2001). Although numerous studies based on social force model focused on the general mechanism of pedestrian
behavior, few of them shed light on the application of social force model to pedestrian behavior at signalized crosswalk. Teknomo
(2006) developed a social force based model and analyzed the pedestrian behavior of one-way and two-way pedestrian flow at
crosswalk. It was found that two-way traffic performance reduces significantly as the number of pedestrians increase. Li et al.
(2012) modified the social force model by considering the required space and the critical gaps with turning vehicles, which
makes it possible to describe the stop/go decision to the conflicting vehicles. However, these models, neither fully developed nor
calibrated based on real trajectory data, failed to illustrate the crucial pedestrian behavior at crosswalk.
To fill the gap, the special characteristics of pedestrian behavior at signalized crosswalk are considered in this study. It takes into
account the crossing behaviors such as group evasive and individual evasive behavior with surrounding pedestrians, collision
avoidance with turning vehicles, reaction to crosswalk boundary, and response to signal control, which are all important to

evaluate the safety performance of signalized intersections. Then, a statistical method is applied to parameter calibration. In this
study, the parameters are separated into two groups, measurable and non-measurable ones. The measurable parameters are
directly estimated from the observed trajectory dataset, and the non-measurable parameters are derived by using maximum
likelihood estimation (MLE). Last, the results of simulation scenarios conclude that the model enables to represent the typical
characteristics of pedestrian behavior at signalized crosswalk such as lane-formation phenomenon, collision avoidance with
conflicting vehicles and surrounding pedestrians.
2. Model framework
As shown in Fig. 1, the general structure of the model is based on the decision making level of psychological process. The first
level is strategic level, at which pedestrians make a decision of the intended direction. The second level is operational level,
which is the second step for pedestrians to adjust their walking behavior at the crosswalk. Note that after reacting to the
pedestrian signal phase and deciding to enter the crosswalk, pedestrians will adjust their speeds and directions dynamically
when interacting with other road users and crosswalk facilities.

Fig. 1. Psychological process of pedestrian behavior.
3. Desired direction model
The psychological process of decision-making of desired direction is like the route-choice strategy. As shown in Fig. 2, there are
four OD zones (N1, N2, F1, F2) defined for pedestrian crossing. Accordingly, there are three stages for a pedestrian finishing the
crossing, i.e., from origin to crosswalk entering position, from crosswalk entering position to crosswalk exit position, and last
from crosswalk exit position to destination. It is assumed that the desired entering position at crosswalk is determined once the
pedestrians enter the waiting area and the desired exit position is determined once the pedestrian enter the crosswalk. Thus, the
desired direction at waiting area (stage ①) is defined as the vector pointing from current position to the desired entering position,
and the desired direction at crosswalk (stage ②) is defined as the vector pointing from the current position to the desired exit
position. Since the focus of this study is pedestrian walking behavior at crosswalk, only the second stage is discussed in the
following sections.

Fig. 2. Definition of OD and desired direction.
Based on the empirical analysis of pedestrian trajectory, the entering and exit positions at the edge of crosswalk were found to be
influenced by crosswalk geometry, pedestrian origin–destination movement, previous passing position and the densities of other
road users. Considering that Weibull distribution is able to represent various types of distribution including skewed ones, it is
applied to estimate the desired entering and exit positions of pedestrians. Thus, the probability density function of passing
position distribution is established by using Weibull distribution as shown in Eq. (1) (Zeng et al., 2013).

4. Stop/go decision model
It is assumed that all the pedestrians choose to go when the signal phase turns to green and to stop entering the crosswalk once
the signal phase turns to red. However, pedestrian flashing green (PFG) is a special signal phase which is considered as a
clearance time interval for pedestrians to safely finish walking at crosswalk. In Japan, although pedestrians are not supposed to
enter the crosswalk during PFG by law, many of them rush into the crosswalk without taking care of approaching turning
vehicles, which may cause higher probability to get severe conflicts with the vehicles. Thus, a stop/go decision model is necessary
to present the pedestrian crossing behavior at PFG. According to the pedestrian crossing behavior model by Asano et al. (2013),
the probability of stop/go is related to the walking speed and the distance from the current position to the crosswalk at the onset
of PFG. In the binary Logit-based model, the error term of utility function to choose “go” is assumed to follow Gumbel
distribution and the probability to go is represented by Eq. (2).

5.1. Driving force

5.3.2. Individual evasive behavior
It is assumed that the subject pedestrian will implement a giving-way maneuver (Asano et al., 2010) to the nearest conflicting

Once a valid conflict is identified, a giving-way maneuver will be performed. As shown in Fig. 4(d), the subject pedestrian takes
actions to avoid collisions with the nearest conflicting pedestrian β by adjusting the speed or direction. A lack of giving-way
maneuver may lead to a problem that two pedestrians may stop forever (when TTCP  → +∞ and TTCP  → +∞) due to social force
balance. For instance, when two conflicting pedestrians in opposite directions move in a straight line, the social force balance
may occur if no other disturbances come. Thus, an active mechanism to avoid such balance conditions is necessary. The giving-
way maneuver is flexible and stochastic in the real world. Pedestrians can pass the nearest conflicting pedestrian smoothly by
adopting either accelerating or decelerating maneuver. Meanwhile, direction changing or not is dependent on personal
preference. To reflect the characteristic of the giving-way maneuver, a stochastic adjustment mechanism is developed. Fig. 5

5.5. Attractive force from signal
Pedestrian behavior is also influenced by the attractive force from signal control. Empirical analyses indicate that pedestrian
speeds are significantly higher at pedestrian flashing green (PFG) phase than those at pedestrian green phase (PG). The major
reason is that pedestrians need to finish crossing before the onset of the red signal as soon as possible. Here, it is assumed that if
the pedestrian is still at the crosswalk when the signal has changed to PFG or Red, he/she will accelerate and cross as fast as
possible. As shown in Fig. 7, the magnitude of the force is assumed to linearly relate to the current distance to the desired exit
position. The farther pedestrians are from the desired exit position, the stronger the force will be. However, if the current speed
has achieved the maximum acceptable speed, the force should be zero. Thus, the attractive force

6. Model calibration
6.1. Data acquisition
As shown in Fig. 8, the north crosswalk at Kanayama intersection in Nagoya City of Japan was chosen as study site. The positions
of pedestrians and left-turning vehicles were manually extracted from the video every 0.5 s by using an image processing
software (Suzuki and Nakamura, 2006). The dataset consists of the trajectories of 1352 pedestrians and 124 left-turning vehicles.
In total, 97,200 position samples are available for trajectory profiles based on time series. From these data, all relevant variables
can be derived either directly or by applying finite differences, such as positions 
, velocities 
, accelerations
, distances between pedestrians, and speed angles between pedestrians.

6.2. Calibration methodology
At the early stage, model parameters are commonly calibrated by comparing aggregate outcomes such as speed–density
relationship (Seyfried et al., 2006), or emerging pedestrian movement patterns such as lane formation (Helbing et al., 2000).
However, it is still unclear whether a microscopic model is able to represent individual walking behavior accurately with a
macroscopic calibration method, considering the fact that aggregate outcomes only provide a reasonable average prediction
(Hoogendoorn and Daamen, 2006). Recently, as the calibration of microscopic pedestrian models has been considered as an
important issue, some researchers have calibrated social force models by the maximum log-likelihood estimation method which
is able to describe the parameters in a statistical way (Daamen and Hoogendoorn, 2012, Ko et al., 2013). The calibration
methodology adopted in this study is similar to the one used by Hoogendoorn and Daamen (2006). However, there exist two
major differences. First, considering that acceleration is the second difference of the position, to estimate acceleration will
amplify the error in trajectory processing. Thus, this study uses the raw positions of trajectory rather than the estimated
acceleration for calibration. Secondly, the parameters are separated into two groups, i.e., measurable parameters and non-
measurable parameters, and calibrated respectively.
The developed social force model contains various parameters. As some of them cannot be derived from the observed dataset,
direct calibration is difficult. Here, we adopt a three-stage process to alleviate this problem. As the first step, the parameters such
as visual range, which are measurable but difficult to be identified from the trajectory dataset, are estimated by referring to
related studies. Secondly, the parameters such as relaxation time, which are possible to be identified from the observed dataset,
are directly estimated. Finally, after fixing the above parameters, other parameters, such as the strength coefficients of each force
that do not have concrete physical meanings, are derived by MLE.
6.2.1. Calibration of measurable parameter
Altogether, there are seven measurable parameters in the developed social force model, i.e., angle of visual range (θ), radius of
visual range (R), desired speed (
), relaxation time (τ ), maximum acceptable speed (v
), velocity change (Δv) and direction
change (ψ). The visual range is defined as the radius and angle at which a given standard object can be seen with unaided eyes.
Due to the limitation of data collection, it is difficult to directly estimate the visual range based on the trajectory dataset. Here,
according to related studies (Guo et al., 2010, Antonini et al., 2006), the angle of visual range is set as 170° and the radius of visual
range is set as 8 m.
Desired speed is defined as the personal average speed if a pedestrian crosses the road without any disturbance by signal,
boundary, surrounding pedestrians and vehicles. The trajectory data were collected when there were no conflicting pedestrians
and left-turning vehicles at pedestrian green phases and the subject pedestrian must be inside the crosswalk, which guarantees
less repulsive or attractive force influencing the subject pedestrian. As shown in Fig. 9(a), the results show that the average value
of desired speed is 1.6 m/s and the standard deviation is 0.15 m/s.

Relaxation time is defined as the time for a pedestrian recovering from current speed to the personal desired speed without any
disturbance. To reduce the influence of other forces, only the dataset for desired speed estimation was used. However, the
pedestrian speeds still change dynamically due to personal random decision. Here, the relaxation time is estimated as the

than the desired speed, a loop will start up to search forward and find out how many time steps were needed until the speed
recovers to the desired speed. As shown in Fig. 9(b), the average value is 2.2 s and the standard deviation is 0.5 s.

Fig. 10. Flow chart of relaxation time estimation.
Maximal acceptable speed is defined as the maximum instant speed along one pedestrian trajectory. Here, all of the trajectories
are utilized as the estimation dataset. As shown in Fig. 9(c), the average of maximal acceptable speeds is 1.8 m/s and the standard
deviation of maximal acceptable speeds is 0.3 m/s.
Velocity change is defined as the speed difference between the quantities of the current speed and the speed at next time
interval when the subject pedestrian interacts with the nearest conflicting pedestrian in the private sphere. To reduce other
influences, the trajectory data collection meets the following requirements. First, there was only one conflicting pedestrian and
he/she was in the private sphere of the subject pedestrian. Second, no left-turning vehicles exist. Third, it was at pedestrian green
phases. Finally, the subject pedestrian must be inside the crosswalk. As shown in Fig. 9(d), the average value is −0.07 m/s and the
standard deviation is 0.09 m/s.
Direction change is the angle difference between the current speed vector and the speed at next time interval when the subject
pedestrian interacts with the nearest conflicting pedestrian in the private sphere. The data collection condition is the same as the
condition of velocity change. As shown in Fig. 9(e), the average value is 0.03° and the standard deviation is 2.14°.

7. Model performance
To verify the performance, the developed model was incorporated into a microscopic simulation platform (Dang et al., 2012), in
which the vehicle behavior model had been incorporated. The pedestrian behavior is simulated for a large number of interacting
pedestrians confronted with different situations at signalized intersection.
7.1. Individual trajectory in simulation
To analyze the performance of the developed model, one of the pedestrian trajectories was extracted from the observed and
estimated dataset, respectively. The sample pedestrian was simulated while keeping the other pedestrians on their original
trajectories.
As shown in Fig. 11(a), the force from surrounding pedestrians has the most significant influence (an average of about 66.1% of
the resultant force during the whole period) on the pedestrian behavior because the tested pedestrian conflicts with others
during the whole trip. The force from vehicle is zero at the beginning. However, from the 8th second to the 14th second, the
subject pedestrian conflicts with a left-turning vehicle. During this period, the force from conflicting vehicle accounts for an
average of 15.3%. The driving force is around 18.5% of the resultant force during the whole period, providing the power to recover
to the desired speed toward destination. The force from boundary is about 12.4% of the resultant force, which is relatively stable
during the whole crossing process. The force from signal is zero because the crossing behavior happened at pedestrian green
phase.

Fig. 11(b) shows the comparison between the estimated and observed positions of the total crossing trajectory. It was found that

7.2. Passing position at three cross-sections
Fig. 12 shows the distribution of observed and simulated pedestrian trajectories from N2 to F2 at Kanayama North crosswalk.
Note that N1, N2, F1 and F2 denote the OD of pedestrian movement. No significant difference of passing positions is found at the
three representative cross-sections according to two sample t-test for difference of their means at the 95% confidence interval. In
addition, it indicates that the distribution of passing position is closely related to pedestrian OD movements. For example, in the
case of near-side passing position, pedestrians with the origin N2 tend to enter the crosswalk close to the left boundary of
crosswalk (near N2). Then, the peak of the histogram at middle cross-section shifts a little bit to the right side, indicating that
pedestrians have change their speed vectors when interacting with other road users. Finally, the peak of the histogram at far-side
cross-section further shifts to the left since pedestrians are appealed by the destination (F2). This validation demonstrates that
the desired direction model and driving force model can reasonably reflect the pedestrian behavior characteristic at crosswalk.

7.3. Collision avoidance with turning vehicle
To analyze the interaction with left-turning vehicles, the pedestrian passing position at a specified cross-section is chosen for
validation under two following conditions. One is pedestrian vs. vehicle conflicting condition with turning vehicles existing at or
approaching the crosswalk when pedestrians are crossing, whereas the other is no conflict condition. The specified cross-section,
i.e., the central line of the third vehicle lane (from the curb of the exit approach), is chosen for analysis, considering the
pedestrian vs. vehicle conflict occurs most frequently along this exit lane according to the observed dataset. Fig. 13 shows the
comparison of observed and estimated passing positions at the specified cross-section. It is shown that the peak of the histogram
in observed dataset shifts from 0 to 2 m if the pedestrian comes across left-turning vehicles. This phenomenon indicates that
pedestrians are likely to detour to avoid collision with vehicles. According to t-test at the 95% confidence interval, no significant
difference was found between observed and estimated passing positions. It demonstrates that the developed model can well
present the behavior of collision avoidance with vehicles. Note that the peak of the histogram in estimated dataset shifts from 0
to 2.5 m, indicating that the reaction of pedestrian in simulation is more sensitive than that in real situation. Thus, the repulsive
force from turning vehicle still needs to be improved in the future development of the model.

7.4. Self-organization phenomenon
Self-organization is one of the important indicators to verify whether the simulation actually produces real world phenomena or
not. A representative self-organization phenomenon is lane-formation. Due to the impact of various forces from surrounding
environment, the lanes of pedestrian flow will form dynamically and their numbers may depend on OD movement, pedestrian
density and crosswalk width, etc. Two simulation models were applied to examine the lane-formation phenomenon. As shown in
Fig. 14(a), one is route-choice based desired direction model, given that the individual decision-making of desired direction is like
the route-choice strategy. The desired direction is determined by Eq. (1) which is related to various influencing factors such as OD
movement and pedestrian density. The other is non-route-choice based desired direction model. As shown in Fig. 14(b), without
considering any influence from surrounding environment, a uniform random distribution is applied to generate the desired
direction.

Fig. 15 shows the lane-formation phenomenon in the real world and in simulation. It demonstrates that the modified social force
model is capable of reflecting the significant self-organization phenomenon in simulation, though the shape of the formed lanes
is not exactly the same. Comparing to the lane-formation by using non-route-choice based model, the pedestrian group in the
same moving direction develops with a larger size and the length of the formed pedestrian lane appears longer by using route-
choice based model. Moreover, the lane forms easier and keeps a relatively stable shape for a longer period when the route-
choice based model is applied. In contrast, the formed lanes by using non-route-choice based model are smaller and unstable,
which are easier to break down.

Fig. 16(b) shows the distribution of the instant angle between current direction and desired direction. The instant angle was
collected in each time step. Zero degree means the pedestrian move toward the desired passing position straight. The angle
variation can reflect the frequency of interactions with conflicting pedestrians or other factors. According to the simulation
result, most of the pedestrians keep their directions toward the desired destination or only change a small angle in each time
step, which agrees with the statistics in observed dataset. However, the angle variation is larger by using non-route-choice
model. It demonstrates that the interaction among conflicting pedestrians is relatively severe because of the unstable lane-

the desired direction can be reproduced by a reasonable distribution function in simulation, which helps to represent lane-

Fig. 16(c) shows the distribution of the passing time at crosswalk. It was found that there are about 90% of the pedestrians
finishing crossing within 30 s in observed dataset while it is about 86% by using route-choice based model and 76% by using non-
route-choice based model in simulation. The performance of passing time gets improved by using route-choice based model. One
possible reason can be attributed to the fact that the lane-formation is more stable. In reality, it is easier for pedestrians to cross
the road by following someone in lane-formation situation than moving against opposite flows. Therefore, the passing times by
using route-choice based model are shorter when compared to those by using non-route-choice based model.
8. Conclusions and future work
Based on pedestrian psychological process and the social force theory, a modified social force model considering the typical
pedestrian behavior at signalized intersection, e.g., group evasive and individual evasive maneuver, collision avoidance with
turning vehicles, and reaction to crosswalk boundary was developed. Then, the parameters of the developed model were
calibrated in a statistical way. The measurable parameters were directly estimated based on the observed data and the non-
measurable ones were derived by maximum likelihood estimation. Finally, the model performance was verified by various terms,
such as instant position, instant speed, pedestrian trajectory, collision avoidance behavior with conflicting vehicles, and lane-
formation phenomenon. The major contributions of this study are summarized as follows.
(1)
The desired direction model was developed for driving force. Most of the studies so far commonly assumed
simplified directions of OD as desired directions and the influencing factors such as crosswalk geometry and
pedestrian density were seldom considered. This study developed a desired direction model by estimating
the desired exit position at the edge of crosswalk, which enables to represent the pedestrian maneuver
toward a predetermined position more realistically. It is considered as an important step to approximate the
pedestrian trajectory at crosswalk. The verification of passing positions at three cross-sections showed no
significant difference between estimated and observed trajectories, which demonstrated that the desired
direction model and driving force model can reasonably reflect the pedestrian behavior characteristic at
crosswalk.
(2)
The typical stop/go decision-making behavior at PFG phase was incorporated, whereas the classical social
force model paid less attention upon this. The model was intended to represent the risk-taking crossing
behavior at PFG, which is crucial to pedestrian safety assessment at signalized intersections.
(3)
Not only repulsive effect, but also attractive effect were considered in the force from crosswalk boundary.
Considering that the crosswalk boundary is different from a rigid wall, pedestrians may walk outside the
boundary when the conflicts with other pedestrians become severe due to increasing density. Classical social
force from the border only presents the repulsive effect, which makes the pedestrians always inside a
walkway. The newly developed force from crosswalk boundary not only maintains the repulsive effect, but
also provides an attractive effect to those pedestrians outside the crosswalk.
(4)
A giving-way maneuver was modeled for individual evasion. The stochastic adjustment mechanism by speed
changing and direction changing was developed, which enables to effectively solve the problems in
simulation that two conflicting pedestrians may collide or stop forever (TTCP  → +∞ and TTCP  → +∞) due to
social force balance.
(5)
The force from conflicting left-turning vehicle was modeled. Avoiding collision with conflicting vehicle is one
of the typical pedestrian behavior at crosswalk. Similar to the repulsive force from pedestrian group, an
elliptical force field was assumed to describe the force generated by the nearest left-turning vehicle. The
verification of collision avoidance with turning vehicle showed that the distribution of passing position at a
specified cross-section is able to reflect the repulsive effect by conflicting vehicle.

(6)
The attractive force from PFG or Red phase was developed. This force is generated when the signal changes to
PFG or Red. The magnitude of the force was assumed to linearly relate to the current distance to the desired
exit position. The farther pedestrians are from the desired exit position, the stronger the force will be. This
attractive force can reflect the real phenomenon that pedestrians prefer to finish crossing as soon as possible
when he/she perceives potential risk.
(7)
In addition, this study provides a more detailed procedure for model calibration by distinguishing measurable
and non-measurable parameters. It was demonstrated to be an effective way to achieve favorable calibration
results.
(8)
The analysis of lane-formation phenomenon demonstrates that the modified social force model is capable of
reflecting the typical characteristic of self-organization in simulation. Moreover, the desired direction model
proposed by this study helps to improve the pedestrian flow performance at crosswalk.
However, there are still several problems needing further attention in future work. For example, the reaction of pedestrians to
vehicles is more sensitive in simulation than in real world. In addition, the pedestrian heterogeneous characteristics such as age,
gender, and partner relationship should also be considered as the influencing factors of crossing behavior. These limitations will
be improved by a more elaborate modeling approach and a more strict calibration procedure in our future work.
